subject: ap-calculus-bc
theme: The first and second derivatives reveal where a function increases, decreases, has extrema, changes concavity, and has inflection points — all without a graph.

core_concepts.title: Mean Value Theorem
core_concepts.detail: If $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$, then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that $f'(c) = \frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}$. The instantaneous rate must equal the average rate at least once.

core_concepts.title: First Derivative Test
core_concepts.detail: At a critical point where $f'$ changes from positive to negative, $f$ has a local max. Where $f'$ changes from negative to positive, $f$ has a local min. No sign change means no extremum.

core_concepts.title: Candidates Test
core_concepts.detail: On a closed interval $[a,b]$, absolute extrema occur at critical points or endpoints. Evaluate $f$ at all critical points in $(a,b)$ and at both endpoints; the largest is the absolute max, the smallest is the absolute min.

core_concepts.title: Concavity and Inflection Points
core_concepts.detail: If $f''(x) > 0$, the graph is concave up (opens like a cup). If $f''(x) < 0$, concave down. An inflection point occurs where $f''$ changes sign — not just where $f'' = 0$.

core_concepts.title: Second Derivative Test
core_concepts.detail: At a critical point $c$ where $f'(c) = 0$: if $f''(c) > 0$, then $f$ has a local min; if $f''(c) < 0$, local max; if $f''(c) = 0$, the test is inconclusive.

core_concepts.title: Optimization
core_concepts.detail: To optimize a quantity: write it as a function of one variable, find critical points by setting the derivative to zero, then classify using the First or Second Derivative Test or Candidates Test.

formulas.name: Mean Value Theorem
formulas.description: Guarantees that at some point c in (a,b), the instantaneous rate of change equals the average rate of change over the interval. Requires continuity on [a,b] and differentiability on (a,b).

formulas.name: First Derivative Test (sign chart)
formulas.katex_string: f' \text{ changes } +\to- \Rightarrow \text{local max};\quad f' \text{ changes } -\to+ \Rightarrow \text{local min}
formulas.description: Check the sign of f' on either side of each critical point. A sign change determines whether the critical point is a max, min, or neither.
formulas.example: For $f(x) = x^3 - 3x$: $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 3(x-1)(x+1)$. $f'$ goes $+\to-$ at $x = -1$ (local max) and $-\to+$ at $x = 1$ (local min).

formulas.name: Second Derivative Test
formulas.katex_string: f'(c) = 0 \text{ and } f''(c) > 0 \Rightarrow \text{local min};\quad f''(c) < 0 \Rightarrow \text{local max}
formulas.description: A quicker way to classify critical points when the second derivative is easy to compute. If f''(c) = 0, fall back to the First Derivative Test.

exam_tip: On FRQs, always justify extrema conclusions with the specific sign change of $f'$ (First Derivative Test) or the sign of $f''$ (Second Derivative Test). MVT requires stating both continuity on $[a,b]$ and differentiability on $(a,b)$.
